headline: Conversion Out of Islam: Zero Legal Exits in Peninsular Malaysia Since 2018
context: Shariah courts in Peninsular Malaysia have not approved a single application to leave Islam since 2018, despite receiving an estimated 135 applications.

cards.big: 135 applications to leave Islam in Peninsular Malaysia since 2018. Zero approved.
cards.sub: Applicants enter a process with no realistic prospect of success.

cards.big: Article 11(1) guarantees every person the right to profess and practise their religion.
cards.sub: The Federal Court has not ruled definitively on whether this includes the right to change religion.

cards.big: Sabah and Sarawak allow apostasy applications through a defined process.
cards.sub: In Sabah, 42 exits were approved since 2018. Constitutional rights are not applied uniformly.

cards.big: Counselling periods imposed by Shariah courts average 2-5 years.
cards.sub: Applicants describe the process as indefinite detention in bureaucracy.

cards.big: A right that exists in the constitution but not in any courtroom is not a right. It is a theoretical concept.
Freedom of religion must include freedom to change religion, or it is compulsion by another name. The Sabah model shows this is constitutionally possible within Malaysia.
